original_abstract: The traditional use of planar (hetero)aromatic rings in drug discovery often entails suboptimal physicochemical properties and metabolic vulnerability. Consequently, $sp^3$-hybridized caged hydrocarbons—most notably [1.1.1]-bicyclopentanes (BCPs), [2.1.1]-bicyclohexanes (BCHs), [3.1.1]-bicycloheptanes (BCHeps), and cubanes—have emerged as essential bioisosteres capable of mimicking various arene substitution patterns while improving solubility and metabolic stability. This review highlights recent breakthroughs in the light-driven synthesis and functionalization of these high-value 3D scaffolds. We examine a wide array of photochemical strategies, ranging from visible-light photocatalysis and energy transfer (EnT) manifolds to direct excitation and electron donor-acceptor (EDA) complex activation. Particular focus is given to the radical-mediated opening of propellanes, the precise installation of functional groups at bridgehead and bridge positions, and the development of stereoselective and DNA-compatible methodologies. By providing a comprehensive overview of the current synthetic landscape and mechanistic underpinnings, we also discuss remaining challenges and future opportunities for these rigid, saturated frameworks in medicinal chemistry and materials science.
humanized_abstract: Three dimensional (3D) cage structures comprising $sp^3$-hybridized carbon atoms, specifically [1.1.1]-bicyclopentanes (BCPs), [2.1.1]-bicyclohexanes (BCHs), [3.1.1]-bicycloheptanes (BCHeps) and cubanes, have become valuable bioisosteres for planar heteroaromatic rings in drug development, due to their improved solubility and reduced metabolism. In this review, we summarize recent accomplishments in light-driven synthesis and modification of these three-dimensional cage molecules. A variety of photochemical methods such as visible light photocatalysts and energy transfer (EnT) pathways, direct photoexcitation as well as electron donor-acceptor (EDA) complex activation are discussed. Key features include the development of photochemical methods that yield the propellanes can be opened by radicals to afford BCPSs, the site-selective functionalization of the bridgehead and bridge sites, as well as the design of highly stereoselective and DNA-compatible photochemical reactions. In addition, the challenges and potential applications of 3D cage molecules in the field of medicinal chemistry and materials science will be discussed, in order to summarize the future development of the cage molecules.

Key features include the development of photochemical methods that yield the propellanes can be opened by radicals to afford BCPSs, the site-selective functionalization of the bridgehead and bridge sites, as well as the design of highly stereoselective and DNA-compatible photochemical reactions.
